text.title: Domain-Driven Design (DDD) Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every arch-pattern-ddd application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Every bounded context MUST have its own ubiquitous language documented. Terms that exist in multiple contexts MUST be explicitly mapped in the context map with their translation rules. Never share a single model across context boundaries — cross-context references use IDs only.
rule.rationale: Shared models across contexts create coupling that forces coordinated changes across teams and contexts. Context isolation is the key mechanism that allows bounded contexts to evolve independently.

rule.statement: Aggregates MUST reference each other by ID only, never by direct object reference. Cross-aggregate operations MUST NOT be wrapped in a single database transaction.
rule.rationale: Direct object references create hidden transactional coupling. Each aggregate is a consistency boundary; cross-aggregate consistency is achieved through eventual consistency via domain events.

rule.statement: One aggregate = one transaction boundary. A use case that modifies two aggregates MUST use domain events or a Saga/process-manager pattern for cross-aggregate coordination.
rule.rationale: Multi-aggregate transactions in a single database transaction are a common DDD mistake. They scale poorly and blur the consistency boundaries.

rule.statement: All access to entities within an aggregate MUST go through the aggregate root. External code MUST NOT hold references to child entities.
rule.rationale: The aggregate root enforces all invariants for the cluster. Bypassing it via direct child entity access can leave the aggregate in an invalid state.

rule.statement: When the aggregate root is deleted, all entities and value objects within the aggregate MUST be deleted.
rule.rationale: The aggregate root is the lifecycle controller for the entire cluster. Orphaned child entities represent data integrity violations.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
